topic: Modules - Re-exports as Public API Design

turns.content: Hey, I’m trying to understand module re-exports in Rust. Can you explain what pub use inner::Type does? It reminds me of how I organize files in my JavaScript projects—do they serve a similar purpose?

Good comparison! In Rust, `pub use` is like making an exported door to your module’s contents. When you write `pub use inner::Type`, it exposes `inner::Type` under the current module’s namespace. Imagine a file system: if I have `/lib/outer.rs` and inside it I say `pub use inner::MyStruct;`, then any code importing from `lib` can just use `MyStruct` directly, without needing to know about the nested `inner`. It’s a way of flattening your module hierarchy so consumers don’t have to remember long paths.
Makes sense. But wait—I’m confused now. If I do that, won’t all my inner modules become visible? That could clutter the API surface, right?

Exactly! To keep things tidy and hide implementation details from end users, you can selectively re-export only what’s necessary. For example, in `lib.rs`, you might write: `pub use inner::MyStruct; pub use inner::my_function;` to expose just those parts of the module. This is like sharing a few select files in your file system instead of everything.
Ah, so I’d put all my public API stuff in `lib.rs`, then selectively use things inside it?

Yes! And for internal re-exports that you don’t want to expose outside the crate—say, helper modules used by multiple parts of your codebase—you can still use `pub(crate) use`. That’s like putting common utilities inside a hidden directory so they’re only accessible internally.
And for versioning my API, would I just keep changing the module names to something like `v1` and re-export them?

Exactly! For example: you can have a stable v1 API that everyone depends on, while evolving features live in newer modules. Then you might write `pub use version::v1::{MyStruct, my_function};` to expose the stable API without changing users’ import paths.
That’s great! I feel like I get it now. So with pub use and wildcard re-exports, I can flatten the module hierarchy while still hiding implementation details—just like my JavaScript imports!

Exactly! And don’t forget that inline documentation is crucial too; you want to make sure all your exports have good comments so consumers know what they’re dealing with. That’s one of Rust’s strengths: clear, well-documented APIs.
